hints.text: Speed signs say **"SPEED LIMIT"** (Canada = "MAXIMUM"); **miles**, feet/inches; limits often end in 5
Wordy signs (railway crossing spelled out); **US English** (center, color)
**"No Passing Zone"** yellow triangle = US only; rectangular junction signs (Canada = diamond)
Yellow centre + white outer lines; **double yellow** common (v. rare in Europe)
White+orange **fibre-optic markers** ~unique to US; rumble strips common
Trailer/mobile homes rural (esp. SE); **Baptist churches** common
US+Canada: post mailbox w/ flag; wooden poles + **cylindrical transformers**; short plates (no blue strip); overhead signal bars; long-nose semis (Europe = cabover)
Gen2 rare → if Gen2 in N. America likely **Canada** (except Alaska, some Hawaii); Gen3 mirror = **single peak** (Canada = two peaks); NA Gen3 usually **no antenna**
East of Missouri R. = denser pop. & greener; West = mountains, drier
Deep South (OK→SC) **reddish soil**; SW Four Corners red mesas/tablelands
PNW (WA/OR/N.CA coast) **temperate rainforest**; Great Plains flat, few trees
New England architecture (shingle/wood panel, porch, high roof) + **three-deckers** → Northeast
Interstates: **even = W–E** (low #s at Gulf/Mexico), **odd = S–N** (low #s at West Coast); US Highways numbered inversely
~half of states have **no front plate**; each state has a unique plate design (some readable through blur)
3-digit **area codes** (not clustered but learnable); each state has its own **flag** (distinct ones appear often)
Appalachians (low forested mtns) → east; Rockies → MT–NM; Great Basin (mtns + dry shrubby plains) → NV/UT
**Saguaro cacti** (tall tubes w/ branching arms) → southern Arizona, Sonoran border
Utility poles w/ **3 yellow stripes** at the base (most of CA, exc. Modoc/Lassen NE)
**Front Range**: a N–S mountain ridge flanking Denver/Boulder/Colorado Springs, plains to the east
Central FL (Orlando↔Lakeland) = the only real **rolling hills**, lakes + groves; jungle-straight roads far south
**Square-reflector bollard** (also UT/AZ) — Idaho appears with both 1 and 2 bolts
**Concrete paving** on ordinary rural roads much more common than elsewhere (ignore bridges/highways/cities)
**White reflectors** on rural signposts (esp. stop signs) — unique to Kansas
Uniquely-shaped **rumble strips**: thin, long recessed lines on the very outside road edges
**Shotgun houses** (very narrow 1-storey wood) → New Orleans area (also Houston, Louisville, Mississippi)
Stoplights & signs **hang from wires** across big intersections, often in yellow boxes
**Yellow signs w/ vertical black triangles** before bridges at major river crossings & guardrail ends
County highways use a **letter system (A–ZZZ)** — Missouri signs lack the word 'county' (Wisconsin has it)
**Sandhills**: rolling grass-covered dunes in the central-north of the state
Bollard = **white vertical-rectangle reflector w/ black outline** on a thin stick
White metal **"E-2" sign** beside the road, typically at bridge ends
Bollard with **3 circular reflectors**; Pueblo-Revival adobe (flat roofs, rounded, sand-brown) buildings
**Square guardrails** (long top bar on square legs) dominant here (also Wyoming)
Street signs showing **street numbers + arrows** alongside the name
**Green sign, 3 rows of numbers** on the back of rural stop signs at intersections
Speed signs often say just **"SPEED"** (not "SPEED LIMIT"); bollard = very long rectangular reflector on top
White intersection signs w/ the **converging state-route number** on top; **keystone** route markers
**White diamond reflector** bollard, usually **2 bolts** (Montana = 1 bolt); Black Hills (conifers) far west
**Yellow/red bands** ('ketchup & mustard') around signposts; grainy salt-and-pepper roads; Hill Country oaks W of Austin
Bollard = **white square reflector atop a black rectangle** (unique); streets named like **"600 South"**
Small **green signs, 3 rows of numbers**, 2nd row starts with **0** (New York's starts with a NYSDOT district #)
Bollard = white/brown post, **white rectangle on front + two small squares on back**; golden wheat fields east
Street signs with a **fraction-in-a-circle** symbol
Simple **white bollards w/ black reflectors** (unique); green **ATV Route** signs; orange rectangle+diamond pole plate (also MI UP)
Bollard: dark post, white circular reflector w/ post rising **above** it; top sometimes painted **white/grey** (unique)
**Interstate 19** (Tucson → Mexico) is the only US highway signed in **kilometres** (speed still MPH)
**Ford Bronco** (unblurred black roof, red hood) on unpaved roads = TransAmerica Trail promo, OR→NC
**Devil's Tower** (lone butte, NE Wyoming) visible for miles; Badlands/Theodore Roosevelt = sand-brown eroded rock
